title: Cherokee Crossroads
description: Walk in the shoes of a Cherokee Nation leader in 1830 as your community faces an impossible choice.

metadata.title: Cherokee Crossroads
metadata.description: Walk in the shoes of a Cherokee Nation leader in 1830 as your community faces an impossible choice.
metadata.author: Branching Scenarios Team

reflection_questions: What factors most influenced your decision as a leader?
How did this scenario change your perspective on historical forced displacement?
reflection_prompts: Consider the economic, cultural, and human costs of each option.
Think about what decision-makers knew — and did not know — in 1830.

scene-council.title: The Council Fire
scene-council.description: It is 1830. President Andrew Jackson has signed the Indian Removal Act. As a council leader of the Cherokee Nation you must advise your people. Two factions press for opposite paths.
scene-council.narration: The fire crackles as elders and young warriors await your counsel. Every choice carries consequences you cannot fully foresee.

choices.text: Support legal resistance — challenge the Act in U.S. courts
Begin negotiating terms of relocation to protect lives now

scene-aftermath.title: History Decides
scene-aftermath.description: The Cherokee Nation wins Worcester v. Georgia (1832) in the Supreme Court — removal is ruled unconstitutional. But President Jackson refuses to enforce the ruling. A minority faction signs the Treaty of New Echota in 1835 without full council authority; the Senate ratifies it by a single vote. Both paths converge: regardless of your choice, U.S. troops begin the forced march in 1838.
scene-aftermath.narration: Sometimes structural injustice constrains even the most courageous leadership. What matters is how you care for your people along the way.

scene-trail.title: The Long Road West
scene-trail.outcome_message: In 1838–39, over 16,000 Cherokee men, women, and children are forced to march 1,000 miles to Indian Territory in present-day Oklahoma. Approximately 4,000 die from disease, exposure, and exhaustion. The path you chose shaped the quality of leadership your community experienced — but not the destination imposed upon them.
